local_names.romanized_local: Hua sheng (Mandarin); Kacang tanah (Malay); Vērkkaṭalai (Tamil); Mūṃgaphalī (Hindi)

food_subgroup: Nuts and seeds — plain or lightly salted

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301403 represents peanut (whole); plain/lightly salted preparation variant not separately coded. Closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical snack serving of plain peanuts is approximately 30 g (about a small handful), providing roughly 170 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Peanuts, all types, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 16087)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced from USDA SR Legacy entry for raw peanuts (all types). Sodium adjusted upward to reflect light salting (~200 mg/100 g) consistent with lightly salted commercial peanut products per USDA Branded Food data. Core macros and micronutrients from SR Legacy raw peanut entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Peanuts have a well-established GI of approximately 14 (range 7–23) as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables published in Diabetes Care. GI = 14 used as the central estimate. GL per 30 g serving = (14 × 4.83 g available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 0.68, rounded to 0.7.

hypertension.guidance: Lightly salted peanuts contribute approximately 60 mg sodium per 30 g serving; individuals with hypertension should choose unsalted varieties and limit portion size to manage daily sodium intake within the recommended 2000 mg/day threshold.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (GI 14) and high fibre and protein content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Peanuts have a very low GI of 14 and a negligible glycemic load per serving, making them a suitable snack for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; their high protein (25.8 g/100 g) and dietary fibre (8.5 g/100 g) further support postprandial glucose control, though caloric density warrants portion awareness.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat content; zero cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Peanuts are rich in heart-healthy monounsaturated fats (24.4 g/100 g) and polyunsaturated fats (15.6 g/100 g) with zero cholesterol, and regular moderate consumption has been associated with reduced LDL cholesterol and cardiovascular risk in epidemiological studies; however, the high caloric density requires portion control.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Plain peanuts (also known as groundnuts) are the edible seeds of Arachis hypogaea, a legume cultivated widely across South Asia, Southeast Asia, Africa, and the Americas. In the Singaporean and Indian food context, plain or lightly salted peanuts are commonly served as a snack, appetiser, or accompaniment to beverages. The peanuts may be consumed raw, dry-roasted, or oil-roasted, and are lightly salted to enhance flavour. Their texture is firm and slightly crunchy when roasted, with a rich, nutty, and mildly sweet flavour profile. Nutritionally, peanuts are energy-dense (567 kcal/100 g) and are an excellent source of plant-based protein (25.8 g/100 g), healthy unsaturated fats (predominantly monounsaturated and polyunsaturated), dietary fibre (8.5 g/100 g), niacin (12.07 mg/100 g), folate (240 mcg/100 g), magnesium, phosphorus, and vitamin E. They have a very low glycemic index (GI 14), making them highly suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The absence of cholesterol and the favourable fatty acid profile support cardiovascular health. Individuals with hypertension should prefer unsalted varieties. Peanuts are a major allergen and must be clearly declared on menus and packaging. They are naturally gluten-free, vegan, and halal.
common_user_queries: What is the GI of peanuts?
Are peanuts healthy for the heart?
Can I eat peanuts if I have high blood pressure?
What is the protein content of peanuts?
What are the nutrients in groundnuts?
How much fat is in peanuts?

confidence_reason: Core macros and micronutrients sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy (FDC ID 16087) for raw peanuts, a primary approved source. GI sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed international GI tables. Sodium adjusted to reflect light salting based on USDA Branded Food data. All values are well-established and consistent across multiple approved sources.
notes: Sodium_mg is set to 200.0 mg/100 g to reflect light salting as described in the food record; raw unsalted peanuts contain approximately 18 mg/100 g sodium per USDA SR Legacy. The 200 mg/100 g estimate is consistent with lightly salted commercial peanut products in USDA Branded Food data. Trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy; peanuts contain no industrially produced trans fats. Cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; peanuts are a plant food and contain no cholesterol. Vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for roasted/processed peanuts. Vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy. Vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; peanuts are a plant food and contain no B12. Vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy. Monounsaturated_fat_g (-1 sentinel not used; value 24.4 reported). Polyunsaturated_fat_g (-1 sentinel not used; value 15.6 reported). All -1 sentinel values in the per_100g block represent nutrients not separately reported in the primary USDA SR Legacy entry for this food item. GI glycemic_load_per_serving calculated as: GI (14) × available carbohydrate per 30 g serving (4.83 g) / 100 = 0.68, rounded to 0.7. Food is served at Anjana Kitchen - Six Battery Road on field survey data at SGD 7.50.

improvement_actions_taken: Sourced core macros and micronutrients from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 16087 (approved primary source).
Adjusted sodium to 200 mg/100 g to reflect light salting as described in input record.
Sourced GI=14 from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed international GI tables.
Added mandatory multilingual local names: Chinese (花生), Malay (Kacang tanah), Tamil (வேர்க்கடலை), Hindi (मूंगफली).
Added FoodOn, FoodEx2, and SNOMED CT ontology anchors using only allowed match_type values.
Documented all zero values (trans_fat, cholesterol, vitamin_c, vitamin_a, vitamin_b12, vitamin_d) as confirmed zero in notes.
Added three health context conditions with specific clinical guidance including Type 2 Diabetes.
Wrote LLM-ready natural language description exceeding 200 characters.
Corrected q8 failure: Ensured all entries from aliases_by_language (specifically 'Vērkkaṭalai' and 'Mūṃgaphalī' from romanized) are present verbatim in the flat aliases list.
